column1: # Principle: Type-name words push type-literal values.
# A bare unquoted word that matches a well-known type name (Integer,
# String, Boolean, Number, Scalar, Atom, List, Map, Any, None, …)
# is NOT an undefined word and is NOT a function — it pushes a
# "type literal" Value: a Value whose VType is the named Type and
# whose Data is nil. The recognised set is in
# Type literals are how the language refers to types as values: you
# can pass `Integer` as an argument, store it via `def`, compare
# types, etc. The Data==nil discriminator is what tells handlers
# "this is the type itself, not a payload".
# 1. Type-name words are recognised: Integer, String, Boolean,
#    Decimal, Number, Scalar, Atom, Path, List, Map, Any, None, etc.
#    None is recognised but renders as the bottom-type sentinel.
# 2. Type literals can be `def`-bound and recalled.
# 3. Type literals can be passed to `tag` (and similar [Any] sigs) —
# 4. The rendering of type literals exposes a known QUIRK in the
#    literals render the same as their zero-Data accessors:
#                            and AsInteger returns 0 for nil Data)
#    Other type literals fall through to the default branch which
#    The asymmetry is a known deviation: see the "Deviations" section
#    of the report for the suggested fix.
# 5. SURPRISE: A scalar type literal (Integer / Decimal / String /
#    Boolean) IS accepted by a sig declaring that exact concrete
#    type — match.go's Data==nil rejection at line 233-236 ONLY
#    rejects type literals for TMap and TList, not for scalar leaves.
#    So `tag Integer` lands on the [Integer]-specific overload and
#    the handler (which never reads the value) returns "specific".
#    Likewise `add Integer 1` succeeds — the handler's AsInteger
#    silently returns 0 for Integer (the type literal), 1 for the
#    This is the documented behaviour but is a genuine footgun: a
# 6. To capture a type-name word as DATA (an Atom of the same name)
#    rather than as the type itself, use `quote`. The /q-style
# Type literals are first-class values — they're how the language
# spells "the type Integer" or "the type List" in source. Every
# unions) builds on them. Pinning down BOTH their dispatch behaviour
# AND their stringly-rendered surprise lets a future renderer or
